content.text: Installing a plugin is step one. Knowing how to use it effectively — and how to share it with a team or publish it publicly — determines whether it creates lasting productivity gains or sits unused. This topic covers the day-to-day experience of working with plugins as a user, and the distribution paths available to plugin authors.
Using Installed Plugins

Once a plugin is active in a workspace or project, its effects are immediate: plugin instructions are injected into the engine context on every new goal, and plugin skills appear in the slash-command picker when you start a new goal.
Invoking a skill
Type

in the goal input field to open the slash-command picker. It lists all skills from all active plugins, sorted alphabetically with the plugin name as a prefix label. Click a skill or keep typing to filter by name.
Type / in the goal input to open the skill picker
Start typing the skill name to filter the list
Select the skill — the goal input pre-fills with the template
Fill in any {{placeholder}} fields highlighted in the template
Review the pre-filled goal, adjust if needed, and run

The engine receives the fully resolved template as the goal. If the skill includes connector references, those connectors must be authorized — a connector warning appears in the goal preview if authorization is missing.
Checking which plugins are active

The plugin indicator in the Cowork toolbar shows a count of active plugins for the current context (workspace + project). Click it to see the full list of active plugins, the scope each applies at (workspace or project), and a link to each plugin's manifest.
Active plugin instructions are always on — they cannot be suppressed per-goal
Skills are opt-in — they only run when you invoke them; they do not run on regular goals
If a skill you expect is missing, check whether the plugin is active in the current project scope
Combining a skill with custom goal text

A skill invocation fills the goal input, but you can edit the pre-filled text before running. This is useful when a skill template covers 80% of what you need and you want to add a specific instruction for this run without modifying the underlying template.

Pre-filled by skill /deal-memo:
Prepare a deal memo for {{account_name}}, currently at {{deal_stage}} stage.
Read the account's most recent 30 emails from Gmail...

User edits before running:
Prepare a deal memo for Acme Corp, currently at Q3-Close stage.
Read the account's most recent 30 emails from Gmail...
Also read the meeting notes in the "Acme" folder on Drive and include
a summary of any verbal commitments the team made.
                               ^^^^ added for this run
Editing skill text does not modify the underlying template. The plugin skill remains unchanged — you are editing only the single goal invocation.
Discovering and Installing Plugins
The marketplace

The Cowork marketplace is accessible from Settings → Plugins → Browse Marketplace. Plugins are organised by category (Sales, Legal, Engineering, Research, Operations, Writing, Finance) and by publisher (Anthropic, verified third-party, community).
Each listing shows: description, component count, publisher, version, last-updated date, and install count
The component manifest is visible before installing — read it carefully before activating in a shared workspace
Verified third-party plugins are reviewed by Anthropic for safety; community plugins are user-contributed
Filter by "Compatible connectors" to find plugins that work with connectors you have already authorized
Evaluating a plugin before installing
Before installing any plugin in a workspace, review three things:
Instructions: what always-on directives will this add? Will they conflict with existing project memory or other active plugins?
Skills: are the template goals appropriate for your use case? Test one skill goal manually before relying on it
Connectors: which external domains or APIs will this plugin call? Confirm your org permits those connections

Never install a plugin in a production workspace before testing it in a personal or sandbox workspace. Plugin instructions cannot be suppressed per-goal once active — remove the plugin if instructions are not compatible with your workflow.
Installing a plugin
Open Settings → Plugins → Browse Marketplace
Select the plugin and click Install
Choose the scope: personal, project (select which project), or workspace (admin only)
If the plugin includes a connector, complete the OAuth authorization step
The plugin is immediately active — skills appear in the picker and instructions are live
Sharing a Plugin with Your Team

A plugin you built as a personal plugin can be promoted to the workspace so your team can use its skills. This involves exporting the plugin definition and submitting it to a workspace admin for review and installation.
Exporting a personal plugin
From Settings → My Plugins, click Export on the plugin you want to share. This downloads a plugin definition file (
.cowork-plugin
) that contains the skill templates, instructions, resource files, and connector declarations — but not any OAuth credentials or connector tokens.
Submitting to a workspace admin
Share the exported plugin file with your workspace admin
The admin imports it via Settings → Workspace Plugins → Import Plugin
The admin reviews the manifest (instructions, skill templates, connector requirements) before activating
Once approved, the plugin is available at the scope the admin configures (workspace or specific projects)
The original personal plugin and the workspace version are independent — changes to one do not affect the other
Version management for team plugins

When you update your personal plugin and want to push the changes to the workspace version, export again and submit the new file to the admin. The admin imports the new version — it replaces the previous workspace version and takes effect immediately.
There is no automatic sync between a personal plugin and its derived workspace version
For actively developed team plugins, designate one person to maintain the canonical version and manage the export/import cycle
Large workspaces may prefer to maintain team plugins directly in the workspace plugin editor rather than exporting from personal settings
Publishing a Plugin to the Marketplace

Any Cowork user can submit a plugin for marketplace listing. Marketplace plugins go through an Anthropic review process before they are publicly listed. Once approved, they are versioned, searchable, and installable by any Cowork user.
Requirements for submission
The plugin must have a clear, specific purpose — multi-purpose plugins that cover unrelated domains are typically rejected
All skill templates must be safe, non-deceptive, and not designed to exfiltrate user data
Connector domains referenced by the plugin must be documented in the submission and must match the connector's stated purpose
Instructions must not instruct the engine to override its safety behaviors
The plugin must include a description, changelog, and usage examples
Submission process
Build and test the plugin thoroughly in your own workspace
Navigate to Settings → My Plugins → Submit to Marketplace
Complete the submission form: description, category, usage examples, connector documentation
Anthropic reviews the submission (typically 5–10 business days)
If approved, the plugin is listed in the marketplace under your publisher name
You receive a notification when the plugin is live; you can update it by submitting a new version
Maintaining a marketplace plugin

Once listed, you are responsible for keeping the plugin updated. If Anthropic's guidelines change or a connector API the plugin depends on is deprecated, you will be notified to update the plugin. Plugins with unresolved issues are suspended from the marketplace after a notice period.
Monitor the marketplace publisher dashboard for user reports and compatibility warnings
Version bump any update that changes skill template behavior or instructions — users may have their workflows depend on the current behavior
Deprecation notices should be published in the plugin changelog before removing any skill
Permissions Reference
Action
Personal user
Project owner
Workspace admin
Create a personal plugin
Yes
Yes
Yes
Install a marketplace plugin (personal scope)
Yes
Yes
Yes
Install a plugin at project scope
No
Yes
Yes
Install a plugin at workspace scope
No
No
Yes
Disable a workspace-scoped plugin
No
No
Yes
Export a personal plugin for sharing
Yes
Yes
Yes
Import a shared plugin to workspace
No
No
Yes
Submit a plugin to the marketplace
Yes
Yes
Yes
Troubleshooting Plugin Problems
Most plugin problems fall into one of three categories: a skill is not appearing, instructions are producing unexpected output, or a connector bundled with a plugin is not working.
Skill not appearing in the picker
Confirm the plugin is active in the current project scope — workspace plugins apply everywhere, but project-scoped plugins only apply in that project
If the plugin was just installed, reload the Cowork app — the skill picker updates on next load
Skill name collisions: a project-scoped skill with the same name shadows a workspace-scoped one; check whether another plugin already defines that name
Skills from disabled plugins do not appear — verify the plugin status in Settings → Plugins
Unexpected output from plugin instructions
If your goals are producing outputs that deviate from what you expect — unusual formatting, extra caveats, or different tone — active plugin instructions are the most likely cause.
Open the plugin indicator in the toolbar to see all active plugins
Click each plugin to read its instructions in the manifest
Identify which instruction might be causing the deviation
Temporarily disable the plugin and re-run the goal to confirm
If the instruction is from a marketplace plugin and conflicts with your workflow, contact the plugin author or remove the plugin
Plugin connector authorization errors

If a skill that uses a bundled connector fails with an authorization error, the connector's OAuth token has expired or been revoked. Re-authorize from Settings → Plugins → [plugin name] → Connector → Re-authorize.
OAuth tokens typically expire after 60–90 days depending on the service; re-authorization takes under a minute
If the connector was revoked by an admin (e.g. IT revoked third-party OAuth access), re-authorization will fail until the admin re-grants access
Disabling and re-enabling a plugin does not refresh the connector token — re-authorization is done explicitly in the connector settings
Building a Plugin Library for Your Team
Once a team moves beyond a single plugin and starts accumulating several, a lightweight governance practice keeps the library coherent and prevents conflicts.
Plugin naming conventions
Consistent plugin naming makes the skill picker readable and prevents confusion. A common convention is to prefix skill names with a domain identifier:

/legal-contract-review    — from the Legal plugin
/legal-nda-summary
/legal-risk-flag

/eng-incident-brief       — from the Engineering plugin
/eng-pr-summary
/eng-runbook-draft

/sales-deal-memo          — from the Sales plugin
/sales-call-prep
/sales-competitive-brief

Domain prefixes eliminate skill name collisions across plugins and make it immediately clear which plugin a skill belongs to. The prefix convention also makes it easier to audit which skills are in use by grepping goal history logs.
Plugin ownership and review cadence
Designate a plugin owner for each team plugin — one person is responsible for keeping it updated and reviewing feedback
Run a quarterly plugin audit: identify which skills are being invoked regularly and which are never used
Remove unused skills from active plugins — they clutter the picker and may confuse new team members
When a plugin's instructions conflict with a new project's requirements, create a project-scoped override plugin rather than modifying the workspace-level plugin
Documenting plugins for new team members
The plugin manifest is the canonical reference, but it is written for the engine, not for humans. Maintain a short team wiki page for each plugin that describes:
What the plugin is for and which team uses it
The full list of skills with a one-sentence description of each
Which connectors the plugin requires and how to authorize them
Who to contact if a skill produces unexpected output
The version history — what changed in recent updates
Real-World Usage Patterns
These examples show how teams typically use plugins in practice — invoking skills, customizing per-run, and combining plugins.
Pattern: skill + project memory combination
A plugin skill provides the general template; project memory provides the context that makes the output specific to this team's situation.

Project memory (set once in project settings):
This project is for the Atlas account at Acme Corp.
Account executive: Sarah Chen (sarah@company.com)
Current deal stage: Q3-Close
Primary contact: John Smith (CTO)
Open commercial terms: custom SLA, net-60 payment

Skill invocation:
/sales-deal-memo Acme Corp Q3-Close

Result: the skill template runs with the Atlas project memory
already in context — Sarah doesn't need to re-type account
details in every deal memo invocation.
Pattern: scheduled skill for recurring output
Skills are not limited to interactive goals — you can reference a skill's template text in a scheduled task goal. This combines the reusability of a skill with the automation of a scheduled goal.

Scheduled goal (every Monday at 09:00):
Run the same workflow as the /eng-incident-brief skill,
but for the past week's incidents rather than a specific one.
Read all PagerDuty incidents from the last 7 days.
Produce a weekly incident digest with: total count, breakdown by severity,
top recurring root causes, and three improvement recommendations.
Post to #eng-weekly-digest in Slack.
Pattern: chaining two skills

Invoke one skill, review its output, then use a second skill on the result. Each skill invocation is a separate goal — the output of the first becomes an input to the second (manually or via a Drive intermediate file).

Step 1: invoke /research-brief "Model context windows 2025"
→ Engine produces a structured research brief, writes it to Drive

Step 2: invoke /blog-brief "Model context windows 2025"
→ Add to the goal: "base the brief on the research document
   written in step 1 at [Drive link]"
→ Engine reads the research brief and produces a blog editorial brief
Summary

Plugins in Cowork have a layered distribution model: personal for individual use, team/workspace for shared deployment, and marketplace for public listing. Using an installed plugin is as simple as typing a slash command; sharing it requires an export-import cycle through a workspace admin or a marketplace submission through Anthropic review.
Invoke skills by typing / in the goal input and selecting from the picker
Active plugin instructions are always on — review the manifest before enabling in a shared workspace
Promote personal plugins to the team by exporting and submitting to a workspace admin
Marketplace plugins require Anthropic review; once listed they are versioned and installable by any Cowork user
Workspace admins control what plugins are active at workspace scope; project owners control project scope
